case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: User-provided full protocol with 15 min batch time, 83% isolated yield, openai council benchmark.

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N–H (tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to nitrile oxide)

feed_streams.reasoning: All reagents are premixed in DES; nitrile oxide must be generated in the presence of the dipolarophile to avoid dimerization. No incompatibility between phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate at room temperature — reaction only proceeds at elevated temperature. Single feed stream simplifies pumping of viscous DES.

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to its aci-nitro form (nitronic acid), facilitated by the DES hydrogen-bonding environment at 120°C

Dehydration of the aci-nitro tautomer to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the reactive 1,3-dipole). Water is lost. This step is rate-limiting as it requires thermal activation to form the transient nitrile oxide.

Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide acts as the 1,3-dipole and phenylacetylene as the dipolarophile, forming the isoxazole ring regioselectively (5-phenyl regioisomer favored).

stream_logic.reasoning: All components premixed in a single stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are stable together at room temperature; the reaction only initiates at elevated temperature (120°C). Premixing ensures the dipolarophile is present when the transient nitrile oxide is generated, preventing nitrile oxide dimerization to furoxan. DES is viscous (~50-100 cP at RT) so a single stream minimizes mixing challenges. Concentration: phenylacetylene 0.5 M, ethyl nitroacetate 1.0 M in 1 mL DES.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Single premixed feed. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be co-present when heated because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to furoxan if not immediately trapped by the dipolarophile. No catalyst, base, or additive is needed, so there is no incompatibility concern at room temperature.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches full conversion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, simply cooling the output stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol uses water quench for workup/extraction purposes only, not to terminate a dangerous reaction.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide cycloaddition). The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may require careful pump selection (e.g., syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media). Back-pressure regulator recommended to keep system pressurized above boiling point of any volatile components at 120°C. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is straightforward for a heated coil reactor. Coil ID of 0.75-1.0 mm recommended to balance heat transfer with manageable pressure drop given DES viscosity. Stainless steel coil preferred for thermal conductivity. Uncertainty: exact viscosity of TBAB/EG at operating temperature; may need to verify pumpability experimentally.

final_design_candidate.human_explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal reactor:**
- **Feed stream A**: Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv), ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv), premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent at 0.0667 M
- **Reactor**: Stainless steel coil, 0.75 mm ID, 1.214 mL volume, 120.0°C
- **Residence time**: 13.8 min
- **Flow rate**: 0.088 mL/min
- **Back-pressure regulator**: 5.0 bar
- **Post-reactor**: Cooling to room temperature

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition analogies where nitrile oxides are generated in situ and immediately trapped by dipolarophiles. The 120°C temperature matches the batch protocol and is required for thermal dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to form the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate.

The 13.8 min residence time was derived from kinetic modeling with a 10× flow enhancement factor typical for thermal reactions, then adjusted upward to achieve >85% conversion. The single premixed feed eliminates mixing issues critical for this reaction - the transient nitrile oxide must be generated in the presence of the dipolarophile to prevent dimerization to furoxan.

Stainless steel tubing provides superior thermal conductivity compared to PFA for this high-temperature process, while the 5.0 bar BPR prevents volatilization of DES components and maintains liquid phase operation.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch

- **Concentration reduced** from 0.5 M to 0.0667 M due to flow rate constraints with viscous DES
- **Continuous heating** vs batch heating eliminates temperature ramp time
- **No water quench** needed - simple cooling stops the reaction
- **Premixed reagents** vs sequential addition (acceptable since reaction only initiates at 120°C)
- **Pressurized system** (5 bar) vs atmospheric batch conditions

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

- **Viscous solvent handling**: DES viscosity (~50-100 cP) requires syringe pump rated for viscous media
- **Conversion concern**: Predicted 63% conversion below 85% target - residence time was increased from 6 min to 13.8 min to address this
- **Flow enhancement uncertainty**: 10× intensification factor is class-typical but not experimentally verified for this specific reaction
- **Recommended validation**: Run blank DES at operating conditions for 60 min to verify pump stability and pressure consistency

The design prioritizes safety and operability over maximum productivity, with excellent pressure headroom (99.99%) and laminar flow conditions (Re = 2.5).

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

The kinetics landscape for the 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition candidates shows a consistent τ of 60 min across all candidates, which is significantly longer than the estimated τ_kinetics of 6 min based on a thermal reaction class IF of 10×. ## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal reactor:**
- **Feed stream A**: Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv), ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv), premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent at 0.0667 M
- **Reactor**: Stainless steel coil, 0.75 mm ID, 1.214 mL volume, 120.0°C
- **Residence time**: 13.8 min
- **Flow rate**: 0.088 mL/min
- **Back-pressure regulator**: 5.0 bar
- **Post-reactor**: Cooling to room temperature

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition analogies where nitrile oxides are generated in situ and immediately trapped by dipolarophiles. The 120°C temperature matches the batch protocol and is required for thermal dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to form the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate.

The 13.8 min residence time was derived from kinetic modeling with a 10× flow enhancement factor typical for thermal reactions, then adjusted upward to achieve >85% conversion. The single premixed feed eliminates mixing issues critical for this reaction - the transient nitrile oxide must be generated in the presence of the dipolarophile to prevent dimerization to furoxan.

Stainless steel tubing provides superior thermal conductivity compared to PFA for this high-temperature process, while the 5.0 bar BPR prevents volatilization of DES components and maintains liquid phase operation.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch

- **Concentration reduced** from 0.5 M to 0.0667 M due to flow rate constraints with viscous DES
- **Continuous heating** vs batch heating eliminates temperature ramp time
- **No water quench** needed - simple cooling stops the reaction
- **Premixed reagents** vs sequential addition (acceptable since reaction only initiates at 120°C)
- **Pressurized system** (5 bar) vs atmospheric batch conditions

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

- **Viscous solvent handling**: DES viscosity (~50-100 cP) requires syringe pump rated for viscous media
- **Conversion concern**: Predicted 63% conversion below 85% target - residence time was increased from 6 min to 13.8 min to address this
- **Flow enhancement uncertainty**: 10× intensification factor is class-typical but not experimentally verified for this specific reaction
- **Recommended validation**: Run blank DES at operating conditions for 60 min to verify pump stability and pressure consistency

The design prioritizes safety and operability over maximum productivity, with excellent pressure headroom (99.99%) and laminar flow conditions (Re = 2.5).

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to its aci-nitro form (nitronic acid), facilitated by the DES hydrogen-bonding environment at 120°C

Dehydration of the aci-nitro tautomer to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the reactive 1,3-dipole). Water is lost. This step is rate-limiting as it requires thermal activation to form the transient nitrile oxide.

Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide acts as the 1,3-dipole and phenylacetylene as the dipolarophile, forming the isoxazole ring regioselectively (5-phenyl regioisomer favored).

stream_logic.reasoning: All components premixed in a single stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are stable together at room temperature; the reaction only initiates at elevated temperature (120°C). Premixing ensures the dipolarophile is present when the transient nitrile oxide is generated, preventing nitrile oxide dimerization to furoxan. DES is viscous (~50-100 cP at RT) so a single stream minimizes mixing challenges. Concentration: phenylacetylene 0.5 M, ethyl nitroacetate 1.0 M in 1 mL DES.

chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches full conversion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, simply cooling the output stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol uses water quench for workup/extraction purposes only, not to terminate a dangerous reaction.

chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide cycloaddition). The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may require careful pump selection (e.g., syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media). Back-pressure regulator recommended to keep system pressurized above boiling point of any volatile components at 120°C. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is straightforward for a heated coil reactor. Coil ID of 0.75-1.0 mm recommended to balance heat transfer with manageable pressure drop given DES viscosity. Stainless steel coil preferred for thermal conductivity. Uncertainty: exact viscosity of TBAB/EG at operating temperature; may need to verify pumpability experimentally.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

analogies.summary: This process demonstrates a [3 + 2] dipolar cycloaddition reaction conducted in a continuous flow unit (CFU) with automated pumps. The reaction uses DMSO as solvent with diisopropylamine as base, operating at 60°C with a 20-minute residence time at 0.165 mL/min flow rate, achieving 96% yield. The flow process allows for higher temperature operation (60°C vs 20°C in batch) while maintaining excellent yield, with the batch baseline achieving 100% yield at room temperature in the same reaction time. The continuous flow setup enables better temperature control and process intensification, allowing the reaction to proceed efficiently at elevated temperature with only a minimal decrease in yield compared to the batch process.

analogies.record_id: Yang 등 - 2024 - Safety Guided Process Development and Scale-Up of the Highly Energetic Compound 4-Methyl-1,2,5-oxadi.pdf
analogies.summary: This process involves the synthesis of 4-methyl-1,2,5-oxadiazole, a highly energetic compound, using a nitration or cyclization reaction in a concentrated sulfuric acid/water mixture (63:6.3 ratio). The flow process was conducted in a tubular reactor at 154°C with a residence time of 179 minutes (approximately 3 hours) and a flow rate of 0.12 mL/min, requiring a back-pressure regulator at 47.6 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions. Flow chemistry enabled a significant temperature increase from 65°C in batch to 154°C, resulting in an improved yield from 70.8% to 78.8%. The primary advantage of flow over batch was enhanced safety when handling this highly energetic compound, as the continuous process allows for better control of the exothermic reaction and reduces the inventory of hazardous material at any given time. The high pressure and temperature conditions achievable in the flow reactor also contributed to the improved reaction efficiency.
